title: How Websites Track You Without Cookies
excerpt: Even with cookies disabled, websites can use APIs, network signals, and subtle browser properties to build a profile of your activity.
contentHtml:
Many users believe that disabling cookies or using private browsing mode prevents websites from tracking them. While this helps limit certain forms of tracking, modern techniques allow websites to analyze user behavior without relying on traditional cookies.

1. Browser and device characteristics

Every browser exposes technical details such as screen size, system fonts, platform and feature support. These attributes help websites render correctly but can also be combined into a fingerprint.

2. Network-level identifiers

Web servers automatically receive metadata about your connection, including IP address, connection type and sometimes hints about carrier networks. While this data cannot identify individuals, it can assist in grouping sessions from the same device or household.

3. Local storage and session storage

Even if cookies are disabled, many browsers still allow the use of localStorage and sessionStorage. These storage mechanisms are origin-specific and persistent until cleared. They are commonly used for legitimate purposes, but they can also be used to maintain state across visits.

4. Redirect tracking and URL parameters

Some advertising networks use URL parameters to correlate clicks and visits. These identifiers are passed through redirects until the tracking service records them. This does not rely on cookies and works even in private browsing.

5. Server-side correlation

Even without browser storage, a server can correlate repeat visits using timing patterns, request headers or behavioral signals. These methods do not identify a person directly but can restore state between sessions under certain conditions.

What you can realistically do

Some forms of tracking are inherent to how the web works. However, you can reduce unnecessary data exposure by:

  • Using privacy-focused browsers with strict default settings
  • Keeping your browser and OS updated
  • Avoiding unnecessary browser extensions
  • Using private browsing for temporary sessions

Cookie-based tracking is declining, but alternative methods are becoming more common. Understanding how these techniques work is key to making informed decisions about online privacy.
